findings.description: Page-width table layout is preserved but the HTML introduces much larger row heights and wider whitespace, reducing compactness.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows a dense grid table fitting many rows in a compact page. The HTML keeps the same single-table structure and columns, but rows are much taller and spacing is looser.

findings.description: Text formatting differs slightly, with header styling and cell text wrapping changed in HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a compact centered title and tighter header cells; the HTML title is left-aligned with a horizontal rule and body text wraps more aggressively within owner/facility cells.

findings.description: Second-page layout is preserved functionally, but the HTML expands whitespace substantially and does not mirror the compact final-page presentation.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF contains the remaining rows in a compact table plus a short 'Total Cases: 35' line beneath. The HTML preserves the same content order and table structure but with much taller rows and more vertical space.

findings.description: Final summary line formatting differs slightly from the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows 'Total Cases: 35' as a simple line under the table; the HTML shows the same text but with different spacing and styling relative to the table.
